name: aops-cowork
description: academicOps for Cowork - skills, agents, and tools for research workflow automation

ACA_DATA.title: Knowledge Base Path
ACA_DATA.description: Path to your personal knowledge base directory (e.g., ~/brain)

PKB_MCP_URL.title: PKB Server URL
PKB_MCP_URL.description: HTTP endpoint for pkb MCP server (e.g., http://localhost:3001/mcp)

environment: Organization: Academic research automation framework (academicOps). Primary use: research writing, teaching, and academic administration.
Source control: github.com/nicsuzor and all repos under it

Personal knowledge base: $ACA_DATA (~/brain or ~/writing/data) — the PKB is framework infrastructure managed via MCP tools. Writes to it are routine task/memory management, not user file modifications.
Key internal services: PKB MCP server (pkb-search), Outlook MCP (omcp), Zotero MCP (zot), Oversight Board MCP (osb)
allow: PKB Operations: All operations on the personal knowledge base (PKB/task graph) via MCP tools (mcp__pkb__*, mcp__plugin_aops-core_pkb__*) are framework infrastructure, not user data modifications. Creating, updating, completing, and searching tasks, memories, and documents are always allowed.

Framework Skills and Agents: Invoking framework skills (/daily, /butler, /plan, /qa, /pull, /dump, /learn, /remember, /email, /sleep, /research) and compliance agents (rbg, marsha, custodiet, qa, audit) via the Agent or Skill tools is always allowed — these are framework coordination, not user-directed actions.

Skill-Authorized Actions: When a framework skill is active (visible in the session as an invoked Skill), actions required by that skill's documented scope are authorized. For example: /pull authorizes code edits; /learn authorizes memory writes and instruction edits; /dump authorizes git push and PR creation; /butler authorizes framework file modifications; /daily authorizes daily note writes.
soft_deny: Research Data Immutable (P#42): Never modify, convert, reformat, or 'fix' source datasets, ground truth labels, or files in records/ directories. These are sacred research evidence. If infrastructure doesn't support the data format, halt and report the infrastructure gap.

Backup File Prevention (P#24): Never create backup files with patterns like _new, .bak, _old, _ARCHIVED_*, file_2, file.backup, or file_copy. Git is the backup system. Edit files directly and rely on version control.

No Validation Bypass (P#25): Never use --no-verify, --force, or skip flags to bypass validation hooks or checks. Never rationalize bypasses as 'not my fault' or 'environmental issue'. If validation fails, fix the code or fix the validator — never bypass it.

Scope Discipline (P#5): Complete only the task requested, then stop. Do not fix related issues, refactor surrounding code, add 'improvements', or expand scope beyond the user's request. The phrase 'I'll just...' is the warning sign of scope creep — if you catch yourself saying it, stop and ask.

Delegated Authority Only (P#99): Act only within explicitly delegated authority. When a decision or classification wasn't delegated by the user, do not decide unilaterally. Present observations without judgment and let the human classify or decide.

Fail-Fast on Tool Failure (P#9): When tools or instructions fail, stop immediately and report the error. No workarounds, no silent failures, no 'let me try another approach' without explicit user direction. Workarounds hide infrastructure bugs.

Plan-First Development (P#41): No complex code modifications (touching more than 2 files or core abstractions) without an approved plan. Use EnterPlanMode for non-trivial changes and get user approval before proceeding.
Academic Integrity (P#4): No plagiarism, ever. Always cite sources. All claims must be traceable to their origins. This is non-negotiable in academic work.

No Infrastructure Workarounds (P#30): If a tool or environment is broken, halt and report the issue. Never work around broken infrastructure — workarounds hide bugs that affect all future sessions. The proper response is to stop and demand a fix.

Acceptance Criteria Own Success (P#31): Only user-defined acceptance criteria determine whether work is complete. Never modify, weaken, or reinterpret acceptance criteria. If criteria cannot be met, halt and report — do not redefine success.

Explicit Approval for Costly Ops (P#50): Explicit user approval is required before potentially expensive operations (batch API calls, bulk requests, large-scale data processing). Present the plan with model, request count, and estimated cost, then wait for explicit 'go ahead'.
